why_them: LRAD purchases and Genasys Protect for active-threat coordination are chief-level decisions executed by division commanders.
email_domains_hint: cityname.gov, cityname.org, countysheriff.org — check the site footer or a press release for the pattern

titles: Deputy Chief (if investigations falls under them)

rules: Always highest rank → lowest rank. No skipping command staff. No burying division heads.

why_them: In many counties (esp. California) the Sheriff runs the Office of Emergency Services — one buyer for LRAD and Protect.
rules: Always highest rank → lowest rank. No skipping command staff. No burying division heads.

why_them: The Genasys Protect buyer. The Director owns the decision; the Alert & Warning Coordinator runs the eval; the GIS lead builds the zones.
email_domains_hint: countyname.gov, readycountyname.gov — the county EM directory (linked below) often lists emails directly

titles: Director of Emergency Management / OEM Director

rules: Director first, always. The Alert & Warning Coordinator is your champion — they live with the current system's failures daily.

why_them: Vehicle-mounted LRAD and evacuation-zone triggers are operations decisions; the Chief signs, the Wildland/Operations chief champions.
email_domains_hint: cityname.gov, firename.org — check a press release or the PIO contact for the pattern
rules: Chief first. The Wildland battalion chief is the strongest internal champion in WUI territory.

why_them: Wildfire-mitigation and dam-safety leads own rate-recovered budgets for PSPS notification and downstream warning.
email_domains_hint: utilityname.com — corporate patterns (first.last@) are usually findable on LinkedIn or press releases

titles: Dam Safety Officer / Chief Dam Safety Engineer

rules: Program owners over executives — the Wildfire Mitigation Plan names its owner; start there.

why_them: Where there's no standalone EM office, the City/County Manager and Public Safety Director own alerting decisions.
email_domains_hint: cityname.gov — council agendas show staff emails in the header block
rules: Manager's office opens the door; the preparedness coordinator carries the eval.

why_them: Sovereign buyers with direct THSGP funding — the Emergency Manager and Council leadership decide together.
email_domains_hint: tribename-nsn.gov — tribal government sites list department contacts
rules: Respect the governance path: the EM builds the case; the Council approves. Never route around leadership.

why_them: Venue security directors and city special-events offices own crowd-communication budgets — World Cup money flows through both.
rules: Two doors: venue security and the city's event command. Reference NCS4 standards with both.

why_them: Port, airport, and transit security directors carry federal security-grant money (PSGP/TSGP) and physical alerting mandates.
email_domains_hint: portofx.com, flyxxx.com — leadership pages are standard
rules: The FSO/Emergency Manager is compliance-driven — lead with grant eligibility and mandate language.

why_them: Installation emergency managers and force-protection officers buy giant-voice replacements through O&M lines.
email_domains_hint: .mil addresses — use SAM.gov points of contact on active solicitations instead of guessing
rules: Work through SAM.gov solicitation POCs and small-business offices — cold email to .mil rarely lands.
